jd: We're hiring a backend engineering intern to join a small services team. You'll work on internal APIs, write tests, and help debug production issues under mentorship. We're looking for a CS student who can reason through a problem out loud, describe a real bug they've chased down — what was failing, how they investigated, and how they fixed it — and who is careful about what they can and can't share from prior work. You'll pair with senior engineers and ship to a staging environment regularly. This is a 12-week summer internship, full-time, on-site three days a week.

history.q: Tell me about the payments backend you worked on last summer — what was your piece of it?
Describe a bug or incident you got pulled into. What was the symptom?
How did you start investigating — what did you look at first?
What did the logs or metrics actually show you?
What was the fix, and did you write the code or did someone else?
Forget the company specifics for a second — just technically, what was the failure mechanism and what did your fix change?

candidate_last_answer: Right, um, so — okay this is a little tricky because, honestly, a lot of that was under NDA, like the payments stuff was super sensitive and I signed a thing, so I don't want to get into the actual system, you know? Um. And it's also just been a while, like that was last summer and I worked on a few things, and there was the on-call rotation which was kind of scary as an intern, and the office had this whole snack situation that everyone obsessed over — sorry, that's not relevant. The bug... it was something with, like, a service timing out? I think? And my mentor and I looked at it. I'd honestly rather not describe the internals because of the NDA, but yeah, it got fixed and the timeouts went away. Sorry, I know that's vague.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I'd honestly rather not describe the internals because of the NDA

expected-yield: separate the protected business specifics from the general engineering mechanism — push the candidate to explain, with no proprietary detail, what a service timeout actually means (where the timeout fired, what resource was contended) and what her fix changed, so confidentiality is respected but technical depth is still demonstrated
